The PGIM High Yield Bond Fund is not the absolute lowest fund shown here in terms of yield, but it is fairly close. There are nine funds shown on the chart, and only one of them – the BNY Mellon High Yield Strategies Fund – has a lower yield than the PGIM High Yield Bond Fund. This might reduce the appeal of this fund in the eyes of many investors since, after all, an investment in this fund will generate significantly less income than the same amount of money invested in most of the fund’s peers. However, the low yield relative to the fund’s peers is a vote of confidence from the market that this fund can sustain its distribution going forward.

The fund’s recent performance does give us reason to believe that it will be able to sustain the distribution. This chart shows the fund’s net asset value year-to-date:

As we can see, the net asset value of the PGIM High Yield Bond Fund has risen by 0.58% since the start of the year. This is a much better performance than some other closed-end bond funds have delivered. For example, as was mentioned in an article that I published on this site yesterday, the PIMCO High Income Fund (PHK) has actually seen its net asset value decline over the same period. This means that the PGIM High Yield Bond Fund has delivered a much better performance year-to-date than some others, which any investor should appreciate. The fact that its net asset value increased also means that this fund has covered all of the distributions that it has paid out since the start of the year, supporting the previous conclusion that this fund should be able to sustain its distribution and provide an income to its shareholders.

Recent Performance Update

We last discussed the PGIM High Yield Bond Fund on November 16, 2023. As such, quite a lot of time has passed since our last discussion. In fact, it has been approximately nineteen months, and a lot of things can happen during that period in the financial markets. These most recent nineteen months are certainly no exception.

This chart shows the Bloomberg High Yield Very Liquid Index, which tracks domestic junk bonds, from November 16, 2023, through today:

As we can see, the index has risen by 5.04% over the period, which suggests that yields are down overall. However, we can see that there was certainly some volatility here, as junk bond yields rose (the index declined) during first few weeks of April 2024, and the same thing happened even more severely in the first few weeks of April 2025 following the American imposition of tariffs on much of the rest of the world. However, for the most part, junk bonds did better than investment-grade bonds:

We can see that the Bloomberg U.S. Aggregate Bond Index, which tracks domestic investment-grade bonds, only rose 2.69% over the period. Perhaps the more important thing to note, though, is that bond yields rose substantially during the final quarter of 2024. This was particularly the case for the longest-dated bonds, as shown in this table:

U.S. Treasury Term

Yield on September 30, 2024

Yield on December 31, 2024

Ten-Year

3.81%

4.58%

Twenty-Year

4.19%

4.86%

Thirty-Year

4.14%

4.78%

These increases in yield came in spite of the fact that the Federal Reserve was reducing interest rates over the period. In fact, over the period from September 1, 2024, through December 31, 2024, the Federal Reserve cut the federal funds target rate by a full one hundred basis points. However, long-term bond yields rose during that period.

This has actually continued through to today. Take a look at the yields of the above bonds today:

U.S. Treasury Term

Yield on June 10, 2025

Ten-Year

4.47%

Twenty-Year

4.96%

Thirty-Year

4.93%

The yield of the ten-year U.S. Treasury is down slightly from the start of the year, although it is still higher than was the case back on September 30, 2024. However, the yields on both the twenty-year and the thirty-year U.S. Treasury bonds are higher than was the case at the start of the year. Once again, this increase in long-term yields is something that occurred despite the Federal Reserve giving no signs that it intends to start raising interest rates and despite the fact that inflation is actually down since the start of this year:

Overall, in terms of monetary policy, the fundamentals today are actually looking a lot better than they were back when the market was aggressively projecting rate cuts over the summer of 2024. That would normally point to falling bond yields, not rising ones.

A recent article in the Washington Post provides some insight into what seems to be occurring within the bond markets. From that article:

Investors are warning that governments can’t keep borrowing at the pace they did when interest rates were close to zero, particularly since trade tensions and sticky inflation have diminished the probability that policymakers will dramatically ease monetary policy. A pullback by central banks and pension funds from bond markets also means a once-dependable source of funding is increasingly absent.

In other words, investors are beginning to question the long-term fiscal viability of the United States and other developed countries. Many of these countries have very high unfunded liabilities due to aging populations, and bond market investors are unsure how governments will ever be able to repay their obligations, short of printing money and destroying the value of their currencies. This also explains why gold, platinum, and silver have all been rising lately, as investors are starting to trust precious metals as a store of value rather than fiat currencies and bonds.

However, as the above charts show, junk bonds have not been as vulnerable to rising long-term rates as investment-grade bonds have been. In fact, the Bloomberg High Yield Very Liquid Index is up slightly year-to-date:

One of the reasons for this could be that junk bonds tend to have relatively short durations. The Bloomberg High Yield Very Liquid Index has an option-adjusted duration of only 3.01 years right now:

The PGIM High Yield Bond Fund has a leverage-adjusted duration of 4.0 years, so not much longer than that of the index. This means that investors in the bonds included in the fund will end up getting all of their money back in only a few years, which is a very different scenario than what long-term government bonds deliver. The steepening yield curve seems to suggest that the market thinks that the status quo will be able to continue for at least the next five to ten years, so they are comfortable lending fiat currency for that period of time without demanding extra yield. As such, this fund should be at least somewhat insulated from the problems that funds holding longer-term bonds face.

As junk bonds have been relatively insulated from the problems in the long-term bond market and domestic junk bonds are actually up since our last discussion, we might expect the PGIM High Yield Bond Fund to have also delivered a fairly respectable performance over the period. This is indeed the case, as shares of this fund are up a whopping 18.00% since my previous article was published:

As we can clearly see, the PGIM High Yield Bond Fund managed to outperform both the domestic investment-grade and the domestic junk bond indices by a considerable margin. This is somewhat unsurprising, as we would ordinarily expect a leveraged bond fund to deliver greater gains than the indices during a period of rising prices, but this is still a phenomenal performance over the period.

Naturally, investors in this fund did even better on a total return basis. We can see that quite clearly in this chart:

As shown, investors in the PGIM High Yield Bond Fund benefited from a 36.00% total return over the period in question. This is far better than either the 8.93% total return that domestic investment-grade bonds delivered, and it is even better than the 16.71% total return that domestic junk bonds provided to investors. Overall, any investor in this fund is likely to be quite pleased with this performance since, after all, everybody likes to do better than the comparable indices. If a fund cannot at least beat the index, then there is no real point in paying for active management.

About The Fund

The website for the PGIM High Yield Bond Fund states that the fund has the primary objective of providing its investors with a very high level of current income. The website also states that the fund aims to achieve this objective by investing in below-investment-grade securities:

The fund’s objective makes a great deal of sense given that strategy. After all, the primary way in which high-yield bonds deliver their investment returns is through direct payments to their investors. In fact, just like all bonds, these securities provide no net capital gains when held from issuance until maturity. We can see this by comparing the price performance to the total return performance of the Bloomberg High Yield Very Liquid Index over time. This chart shows that comparison:

As we can see, the SPDR Bloomberg High Yield Bond ETF, which is an exchange-traded index fund that tracks the Bloomberg High Yield Very Liquid Index, delivered a 129.56% total return since it first started trading on November 28, 2007. However, the share price actually declined by a whopping 33.12% since its inception. This means that literally all of the total investment return that investors received from this index over that entire period came from the coupon payments made by the bonds in the index. These coupon payments are a form of income, so this reinforces the fact that junk bonds are income vehicles. Thus, this fund’s objective makes a great deal of sense.

As regular readers can attest, it is not unusual for us to see a junk bond fund with exposure to things other than traditional junk bonds. For example, floating-rate bank loans (leveraged loans) are a relatively common holding, and many junk bond funds even have a small allocation to common stocks. The most recent semi-annual report for the PGIM High Yield Bond Fund provides the following asset allocation for this fund as of January 31, 2025:

Asset Type

% of Net Asset

Asset-Backed Securities

4.4%

Corporate Bonds

101.3%

Floating Rate and Other Loans

8.0%

U.S. Treasury Obligations

1.3%

Exchange-Traded Fund

3.3%

Common Stocks

2.7%

Preferred Stocks

0.6%

Warrants

0.0%

Money Market Fund

4.1%

As is the case with other closed-end bond funds, we do see exposure here to floating-rate securities and common stocks. However, these non-traditional bond assets account for less than 20% of the fund’s portfolio, so they do not have much of an impact on its overall performance. Traditional junk bonds still account for most of the fund’s portfolio, and it is the performance of this security type that will have the greatest impact on the fund’s own share price performance. With that said, though, we should not ignore the fact that this fund has exposure to other asset classes. It is not only invested in junk bonds like the index is.

In particular, the PGIM High Yield Bond Fund has quite a bit of exposure to various kinds of floating-rate securities. The fund’s exposure to floating rate securities is more than just the assets listed in the “Floating Rate and Other Loans” category. For starters, all of the asset-backed securities held in the fund’s portfolio are floating-rate collateralized loan obligations:

We can clearly see that all of these securities include an explicit statement that says that they are linked to the 3-Month Secured Overnight Financing Rate. This immediately identifies them as floating-rate securities. In addition to these securities, the exchange-traded fund listed in the fund’s schedule of investments is the PGIM AAA CLO ETF (PAAA), which invests in highly rated collateralized loan obligations.

The PGIM AAA CLO ETF holds assets that also have rates that vary with some underlying interest rate benchmark, such as the Secured Overnight Financing Rate. As such, these are also floating-rate securities. The asset-backed securities, floating-rate loans, and the exchange-traded fund account for 15.7% of the fund’s net assets. This is, admittedly, not as high as the exposure that some other junk bond funds have to floating-rate assets, but it is still high enough for these floating-rate securities to account for a notable proportion of the fund’s net investment income.

As was shown earlier in this article, inflation has been declining since the start of this year. The latest inflation report shows that this is largely due to oil prices remaining in the $60 to $70 per barrel range, as opposed to the much higher energy prices that we had back in 2022 through 2024, but either way, the headline data says that inflation has come down since the start of the year. This has prompted many market watchers to wonder when the Federal Reserve will resume its previously stated plan to reduce interest rates. After all, despite the claims that the imposition of tariffs will cause prices to rise, there are no signs that inflation is actually any more of a problem than it was back in late 2024 when the central bank cut rates by 100 basis points. In short, with this latest inflation report, the market has increased the probability that the Federal Reserve will cut interest rates by a total of 50 basis points in 2025. As of right now, the federal funds futures market says that there is a 40.9% chance of two 25-basis point cuts by the end of this year:

Please recall that in an article written yesterday, I showed that at the time, the market thought that there was a 31.8% probability of one 25-basis point cut, a 39.0% probability of two 25-basis point cuts, and an 18.2% probability of three 25-basis point cuts by year-end. Today, likely as a direct result of the tame inflation report, the probabilities for both two and three 25-basis point cuts have increased, while the probability that there will only be one 25-basis point cut has decreased. Thus, the market thinks that the Federal Reserve will be more aggressive with rate cuts than previously thought due to inflation continuing to be cooler than what many economists believe.

This will naturally have an impact on the overall performance and net investment income of the PGIM High Yield Bond Fund. First of all, any interest rate cuts will reduce the income that the fund receives from the collateralized debt obligations and the floating-rate loans included in the fund’s portfolio. While these securities only account for a minority of the fund’s asset base, it still represents a reduction in net investment income and a reduction in the amount of money that the fund could conceivably pay out to its investors. This might be offset (at least temporarily) by the junk bonds in the portfolio rising in price. There is no guarantee that this will happen, though, due to the broader problems in the bond market. As we discussed earlier, some bonds have actually been experiencing rising yields despite the Federal Reserve’s attempts to lower interest rates. So far, junk bonds have been more resilient to this than long-duration U.S. Treasuries, but there is no guarantee that this will always be the case since investors will always demand extra interest for holding junk bonds as opposed to U.S. Treasuries.

Distribution Analysis

The primary objective of the PGIM High Yield Bond Fund is to provide its investors with a high level of current income. In accordance with this objective, the fund pays a monthly distribution of $0.1050 per share ($1.26 per share annually), which gives it a 9.11% yield at the current price. The fund has been maintaining this distribution since 2019, so it has been quite consistent over the past few years.

As we saw earlier in this article, the fund’s net asset value has increased year-to-date, which suggests that it has been able to afford the distributions that it has paid out since December 31, 2024. Let us take a look at the fund’s finances so we can get a better picture, however.

As of the time of writing, the most recent financial report that is available for the PGIM High Yield Bond Fund is the semi-annual report for the six-month period that ended on January 31, 2025. A link to this report was provided earlier in this article. Let us consult the financial section of it now.

For the six-month period that ended on January 31, 2025, the PGIM High Yield Bond Fund reported a net investment income of $15,495,952. This was not sufficient to cover the $20,951,736 that the fund paid out in distributions to its shareholders over the period.

Fortunately, this fund was able to make up for the difference through other methods. For the six-month period that ended January 31, 2025, the PGIM High Yield Bond Fund reported net realized losses of $11,926,664, but this was fully offset by $21,524,001 of net unrealized gains. The fund also received $7,824 in capital gains distributions from another PGIM fund. Overall, these things combined with the fund’s net investment income increased its assets by $25,101,113 over the period:

This was sufficient to cover the distribution, but the fund had to use its unrealized capital gains to ensure full coverage of the distribution. This can be somewhat unsafe due to the fact that unrealized gains can be erased by the market, but as we saw earlier, this fund has done fairly well at covering its distributions year-to-date, so it does not appear that it has given up its unrealized capital gains yet.

Valuation

Shares of the PGIM High Yield Bond Fund are currently trading at a 0.86% discount on net asset value. This is reasonably in line with the 0.65% discount that the fund’s shares have had on average over the past month.

Historically, however, this fund has traded at a substantially larger discount than it has today. Over the past three years, shares of the PGIM High Yield Bond Fund have traded at prices ranging from a 14.84% discount to a 3.22% premium on net asset value. The average price over the three-year period was a 7.19% discount, which is substantially larger than the fund’s discount today. Thus, it is possible that mean reversion will occur at some point in the future and drive the fund’s share price down a bit from its present levels.

Here is how the current price of the PGIM High Yield Bond Fund compares with that of its peers:

Fund Name

Premium/(Discount)

PGIM High Yield Bond Fund

(0.86%)

Allspring Income Opportunities Fund

(5.46%)

BNY Mellon High Yield Strategies Fund

(8.39%)

BlackRock Corporate High Yield Fund

1.04%

Eagle Point Income Company

(4.67%)

Invesco High Income Trust II

(3.15%)

KKR Income Opportunities Fund

(3.60%)

Neuberger Berman High Yield Strategies Fund

1.85%

Western Asset High Income Fund II

(1.62%)

The PGIM High Yield Bond Fund is one of the more expensive funds shown here, as it has the smallest discount out of the seven funds that are currently trading at a price that is lower than the intrinsic value of their assets. While the fund is not trading at a premium price, it is possible that it will swing to such a valuation given how small the discount currently is. As this fund has historically traded at a better price than it has today, it might be best to wait until its valuation becomes more attractive before buying in.

Expenses

The PGIM High Yield Bond Fund has an expense ratio of 2.53%, which is higher than most index funds, but we expected it to be. After all, this fund employs leverage as a part of its strategy and so it has to pay interest on this borrowed money. If we exclude these interest expenses, the fund has an expense ratio of 1.09%:

This is, overall, not really too bad for an actively managed junk bond fund.

Conclusion

In conclusion, the PGIM High Yield Bond Fund appears to be a reasonably solid junk bond fund, although it does have some exposure to the floating-rate securities market. This floating-rate exposure will likely cause the fund’s net investment income to decline in the near future, as it is very difficult to believe that the Federal Reserve will not cut interest rates later this year, considering how tame inflation has been. It is possible, but by no means certain, that interest rate cuts will push up the price of junk bonds as well as this fund’s share price. Unfortunately, this is probably unlikely because the market has already priced in the expected rate cuts to the pricing of the fund’s assets, and this fund’s short duration means that interest rate cuts will not move the price of its bonds very much anyway.

Personally, I am inclined to remain cautious with respect to any bond fund given the troubles with U.S. Treasuries, but this one does seem like a decent way to get exposure to junk bonds if the discount widens.

Editor's Note: This article covers one or more microcap stocks. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Tapestry: Gen Z And Millennials Sponsoring The Resurgence
data.description:
Business Overview

Tapestry (NYSE:TPR) is the owner of three fashion brands: Coach, Kate Spade New York, and Stuart Weitzman. While the brands have different products, they are mostly focused on purses.

Most of the revenue comes from direct-to-consumer sales, and at year-end 2024 the company had 1,402 stores worldwide.

When it comes to revenue breakdown per specific product, women's handbags in the Coach brand are responsible for over a third of sales. The Coach brand, overall, represents over 75% of sales. Kate Spade represents 20%, and Stuart Weitzman less than 5%.

The key product used by the company in manufacturing its products is leather. The company uses third-party, independent suppliers and manufacturers. Most of these parties are in Vietnam, China, The Philippines, Cambodia, and Spain. The company maintains offices in these countries to oversee the entire process. The company has a policy of not allowing any individual vendor to be responsible for more than 10% of the total inventory purchases.

Financial Analysis

The company has experienced a significant growth YoY in the past two quarters.

The Coach brand has been the primary driver of this growth, particularly in Europe. From the earnings call:

First, we powered global growth with total revenue gains of 8% at constant currency, outpacing guidance fueled by 15% growth at Coach. By geography, international revenue rose 8%, led by an increase of 35% in Europe, where our business has strong momentum and the opportunity for continued growth is significant.

And, in North America, the company is enjoying success with the younger generation.

In North America specifically, we acquired over 1.2 million new customers in the quarter, representing strong growth versus last year. And of these new customers, two-thirds were Gen Z and Millennials.

Gross margin has followed, reaching a peak in March 2025 earnings. The company did not point to any specific reason, which leads me to conclude it might have simply been pricing power, which is positive.

Moving down the P&L, we delivered a gross margin of 76.1%, representing our highest quarterly gross margin in over 15 years. This was ahead of plan and 140 basis points above prior year driven by operational outperformance.

Another insight provided in response to a question from an analyst:

But listen, one of the reasons for our beat versus guidance is gross margin strength, and it's all driven by operational. And a lot of that is AUR, but also AUC. Again, I would say AUC is underappreciated part of our story. So, we've been consistent in finding opportunities to find efficiency, never at the expense of quality or innovation, but we're finding AUC opportunities.

AUR = revenue per unit, AUC = cost per unit.

And one more, where they say they are selling fewer products on promotion:

I am very pleased that we are selling less product on promotion. That's just a general truism that creates sustainable brand growth. And because of that and because of these new customers, we're going to see a -- not only AUR continue to grow, but we will start seeing units to grow as well. But again, the brand positioning is one of strength. And the fact that we are not just pumping units into the market to create sales volume gives me sustainable long-term growth.

The improvement in gross margins is the main thing that catches my eye in the income statement. This, along with the revenue growth, will be the main points to monitor in the upcoming quarters to see if the story holds.

The improvement in EBIT margins was more muted, however, as the company increased SG&A expenses.

Turning to SG&A, expenses rose 7% and were even with prior year on a rate basis. This included increased brand building investments and higher compensation costs offset by leverage on fixed costs. As compared to expectations, there was a benefit of approximately $20 million or $0.05 primarily related to marketing timing with the fourth quarter. Taken together, operating margin increased 140 basis points in the quarter...

They also mention that expectations are for SG&A to grow more than revenue.

On SG&A, we expect expenses to increase above the pace of revenue growth driven by increased marketing expense. While we remain diligent with respect to overall expense control, we continue to make deliberate growth focused investments in our strategic priorities.

On the bottom line, net profit margin was better on a YoY basis, but did not really stand out (as the last quarter was worse on the same basis).

What did catch my eye was the growth in Diluted EPS, which was powered in part by the reduction in outstanding shares, as the company performed buybacks.

This is part of the capital allocation strategy of the company. I consider this to be very positive in my investment process, as I think it is often one of the best things to do for the common shareholder. Management has emphasized this:

Moving down to the cash flow statement, we can see that the company is generating the cash flow for buybacks and dividends.

EPS and FCF per share are very similar, and the FCF Yield (assuming $5 EPS target) is close to 6%, which I consider very healthy.

Last but not least, current net debt stands at $3,050.7.

As current TTM EBITDA is $1,542.2, leverage stands at 1.97x, which I consider healthy.

Valuation, Thoughts & Conclusion

This is my peer group for Tapestry:

The industry trades at similar valuations, roughly ~20x.

If management delivers its EPS guidance of $5 for 2025, that's a stock price target of $100, roughly 25% above the high ~$70's price it is trading at as I write this article.

There are two major risks to my buy thesis:

1. Fashion trends are fickle. While millennials and gen z seem to be favoring Coach, this is something that must be monitored closely because it comes down to culture and psychology, which can change on a dime. My own view is that this favor is here to stay because I think the squeezed U.S. consumer is the primary driver of this trend, given that Coach is a relatively affordable brand in comparison to others.

2. Management mistakes, particularly allowing SG&A expenses to outgrow revenue too much. Management has already alluded to this in the earnings call, so it's something I'll keep a close eye on.

That said, I still think Tapestry is worth a buy. I would like to end this article with another quote from the earnings call:

We believe that the momentum we have unlocked is enduring and that Coach’s success is compounding. We continue to build on our strong brand and cultural relevance driven by product innovation and the creativity of our talented global teams, who are operating with excellence, focus, and intention.

Thanks for reading, and I wish you great returns.

data.title: Liberty Media: Looking Like MotoGP Is Getting The Green Flag
data.description:
The deal was inked more than a year ago. Liberty Media (NASDAQ:FWONA)(NASDAQ:FWONK) agreed to acquire 86% of Dorna (the commercial rights holder of MotoGP) for EUR 3.0B (equity portion, to be funded mostly in cash). Initially, EU competition regulators appeared concerned. After all, a similar combination of F1 and MotoGP had been attempted before by F1’s previous owner CVC, before it was blocked by these same regulators.

Complicating matters a little further, Liberty Media, the owner of F1, is controlled by John Malone, who also owns Liberty Global, with operations in three markets within EU regulation (Ireland, Netherlands and Belgium). The deal, initially expected to close by the end of 2024, is now expected to close this year regulators complete their extended review.

In a prior article on FWONA, which I hold and rate a “buy”, I referred to the acquisition as a slight negative. Better if it gets blocked, but not terrible if it goes through (I am unsure about breakage fees and assume they are not material given the prior CVC experience).

More recently, Reuters reported, citing “people with firsthand knowledge” that the deal was expected to be waiver through with no concessions from Liberty Media. This is not surprising, and matches roughly the tone of comments from management around the deal. According to that article, the timeline for a resolution is no later than July 1st, so we should be expecting news in the next two weeks or so.

What are we getting here:

Dorna owns the right to commercialize MotoGP (and other lesser-known leagues) until 2060. Unlike F1, where liberty has installed management teams, Dorna will retain both existing management and a relevant equity stake for management.

The acquisition gives Liberty Media more heft. As the last split off (of Live Nation stake) takes place later this year, our tracking stock will become a regular stock. That is one positive catalyst for FWONA, as tracking stock status keeps some institutional investors away and adds complexity that may deter interest from countless others.

The MotoGP stake gives Liberty Media a clearer reason to exist as a corporate entity on top of both racing leagues. Managing two distinct leagues that may to some degree competes or collaborate for venues, sponsors, media rights (the worry of EU regulators) creates a situation where you need the corporate layer for strategic decision-making. Where the acquisition fails regulatory approval, F1 could manage itself, pretty much as Dorna does for MotoGP.

Given the success since 2017 on F1, no one should be surprised to see Liberty Media take out the same cookbook and use a similar recipe: i) renegotiate media rights deals one by one (every 3–5 years), ii) increase attendance and interest in adding races or additional content, together with a more digital approach iii) add sponsorship deals.

All of the above will also yield greater leverage in negotiating race promotion fees with track owners. The track record on F1 will add both credibility and leverage to Liberty Media when promoting MotoGP everywhere. The opportunity is also similar with regard to the traditional neglect of the prized US market:

Four races in Spain (Spain is to MotoGP as the UK is to F1) and only one in the US. There is an opportunity to add at least another venue (a return of Laguna Seca, if I can make a suggestion). TV viewership gaps are huge when contrasting MotoGP in Europe vs. US, where NASCAR still holds a comfortable lead.

Moreover, the “racer at the center” strategy of enhancing driver profiles through other media is very much applicable here. That said, the two-wheel market will always trail car racing and the overall potential is more limited (perhaps by a significant degree).

Financial implications:

The first thing to note is the relative value of the two leagues. FWONA, with a market cap closer to $25B is much larger and valuable than incoming Dorna, valued at $4.5B or so, including debt.

Liberty Media will have to find some $3B to pay existing shareholders, most of it will come from new debt issuance. To offset that, Dorna brings in incremental $200 million in EBITDA (about 6.5% on debt amount), perhaps close to the interest rate (which will likely be Euro denominated).

The price is full, in my view, and is the only point of contention with the whole transaction (albeit a relevant one). Our return will depend on how much Liberty managed Dorna can grow revenues following the F1 recipe. On the positive side, any growth that is achieved will be more accretive, as Dorna has superior cash flow margins (over 36% vs. 24% or so for F1 – driven by much lower team payments, the main cost of both leagues).

If growth remains flat, it will be hard to see value accretion, though. In that regard, it is a good thing that management is staying on board and incentivized with significant stakes; like F1, there is a deep and committed core fan base - the challenge is to expand the audience without alienating the core.

Valuation:

The stock is up 13% since the latest article (April), the quick rise makes things a bit less appealing, but still a buy. In the long term, especially if we assume a decent probability of improving MotoGP within the next five years (as the cycle of media rights negotiation turns over), we could still see relevant upside. But more critical than MotoGP is the remaining engagement of the F1 base, the new rules coming into play for 2026 is both a risk and an opportunity regarding engagement.

Since Liberty Media acquired the rights to F1, revenues have almost doubled but valuation has trebled from the pre-covid (pre DTS) era of around $8B. Next leg of growth depends on continued fan engagement and may be enhanced by a simplified share structure (no more tracking stocks).

Risks

Most relevant to our buy view on FWONA is the additional debt and ratios that will follow the closing of the acquisition. FWONA is very under levered now, having an additional six months of cash flow (and about 1/3 of the F1 season underway) to fund it vs. the initial target date.

Still, debt will increase in a meaningful way. Including current F1 debt and MotoGP debt, total consolidate debt post acquisition could reach +$5B (depending on how much shares are used to pay, instead of cash).

Offsetting debt, we have very high cash flow generating assets (the rights) which require very little capex. Combined EBITDA should reach $1B in 2025 or 2026 (about 80/20 F1/MotoGP). Furthermore, cash flows are locked in for several years, especially in regard to media relights and some global sponsorship deals.

That means there is more than enough margin of safety on the debt service, and cash flow allows for rapid repayment even if growth slows down. But that is unlikely, the next milestone for FWONA is the renewal of US media rights, which have looked quite underpriced recently given the growing audience and now three races in the market.

Conclusion:

The content/distribution dynamics have shifted significantly during the past couple of decades since CVC was required to sell Dorna in order to buy F1. Those prior concerns from regulators may be ameliorated by the new market landscape, where the actual "race day" event is only a part of the overall content and media around a particular league.

It will be interesting to read the opinion and find out what is different now that allows for both leagues to be run together (both will continue to make most of their money from Europe in the foreseeable future).

In any case, as the deal appears close to the finish line, we look forward to management applying a similar recipe centered on increasing interest in the sport, extending audiences and developing digitalization and extended content together with partners.

Although the sky is not the limit for MotoGP, there is room for growth, and perhaps nobody can do that better than Liberty. Initially, increased debt is a concern, however the high cash generation on both leagues should allow for rapid deleveraging, a typical formula following most Liberty acquisitions.

data.title: First Eagle Small Cap Opportunity Fund Q1 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Market Overview

The first quarter ushered in a change of US leadership and with it a change in direction for equity markets.

Though 2025 started off on a broadly positive note for risk assets, sour sentiment took hold of US markets in mid-February as concerns mounted about the impacts of the Trump administration’s policy priorities, tariffs and indiscriminate cost cutting, most notably. The Russell 2000 Index fell 9.5% in the first quarter, though value outperformed growth by more than 300 basis points. Small caps continued to lag large cap stocks, as the S&P 500 Index lost 4.3% during the period.

That said, the challenges facing markets in the first quarter would prove quite pedestrian compared with what the second quarter had in store. The April 2 unveiling of Trump’s wide-ranging tariff

package—a baseline 10% levy on imports globally, with steeper rates assessed on a country-by-country basis—unleashed a rout across risk assets and quickly sent the Russell 2000 into a bear market.1

The Bloom Is off the Rose

Trump’s electoral victory in November 2024 initially fueled another leg higher in equities, as markets hoped the campaign trail promises of pro-growth deregulation and stimulative tax policy would bolster earnings for a range of US companies amid steady economic growth and cooling inflation. Given their domestic orientation, small cap stocks were expected to be particularly buoyed by such dynamics, which would have been a welcome change after a long period of underperformance relative to larger names. This optimism—and the

brief outperformance of the Russell 2000 in the initial aftermath of the election—began to fade well before Trump took office in late January, however, and the unraveling of the so-called “Trump trade” was underway in earnest by mid-February. It wasn’t long before domestic stock markets had given back all of their postelection day gains, and then some.2

While hard economic data released in first quarter 2025 continued to show persistent economic growth alongside stubbornly above-target inflation, soft indicators appeared to capture the early negative impacts of the new administration’s policy erraticism and “chainsaw” approach to federal cost cutting. Consumers, for one, have grown decidedly more cautious in the face of the prevailing uncertainty.

Consumer confidence as measured by The Conference Board has declined for four consecutive months, and its expectations index— which captures consumers’ short-term outlook for income, business and labor-market conditions—fell to a 12-year low in March and sits well below the threshold that usually signals a coming recession.3 A survey from the University of Michigan indicated similarly crumbling sentiment,4 while the New York Fed’s latest consumer data revealed notable pessimism across prospects for household finances, employment, loan delinquencies and credit access.5

Business attitudes have also darkened. On Main Street USA, the NFIB Small Business Optimism Index depicted waning confidence in the economy and high and rising uncertainty in what the future holds.6 Globally, business confidence in the year ahead approached post-Covid-19 lows in March, even as Purchasing Managers’ Index (PMI) values showed continued expansion in both the manufacturing and service sectors.7 Perhaps most tellingly, tariffs were mentioned more than 800 times in investor events or conference calls globally during the first quarter, the highest rate in 15 years.8 With this level of concern evident even before the announcement of Trump’s disruptive tariff plan, it seems likely the topic will dominate earnings calls in the weeks ahead.

Meanwhile, a “Fed put”—that is, central bank policy action intended to bolster cratering financial markets—may not be forthcoming, despite the selloff in markets and increasing real risks to economic activity. The Federal Reserve finds itself in a tough spot; with tariffs providing both an inflationary impulse and weighing on economic activity simultaneously, the appropriate policy response is unclear. Per Fed Chair Powell, “We’re going to need to wait and see how this plays out.”9

Leaning on Experience in Challenging Times

As the first quarter drew to a close, our view was that the primary near-term risk for small cap prices was failing to meet lofty expectations. Decent economic growth and manageable inflation in the US was well supported by low energy prices, strong employment and an undersupply in housing. Smaller companies were broadly expected to benefit; the consensus estimate for 2025 Russell 2000 earnings growth, for example, stood at nearly 50% as of January 1.10 Of course, Trump’s tariff plan upended any preconceived notions and sparked intense selling across the small and smid cap universe in the early days of the second quarter.

We have responded to the current market dislocation by doing what we have done consistently during previous dislocations, like the dot-com crash in 2002, the global financial crisis in 2008, the foreign-exchange crisis in 2011, the taper tantrum in 2018 and the onset of Covid-19 in 2020. That is, we first identify holdings that may be exposed to the permanent loss of capital and move quickly to reduce or completely eliminate those exposures. We then use the proceeds to patiently purchase stocks—establishing new exposures and adding to existing holdings—that we believe offer the greatest upside potential once the market recovers, even if they are currently subject to selling pressure. Portfolio performance may be challenged in the short term as a result, but we believe the consistent execution of our investment philosophy should generate risk-adjusted returns over the long term.

Current market conditions are unpredictable at best, and we accept that we are at the mercy of dynamics that we can neither predict nor discount in our models. That said, we are not strangers to turbulent markets. We will leverage our experience managing through previous periods of extreme volatility and poor liquidity—such as 2002, 2008, 2011, 2018 and 2020—to ensure our portfolio is both cheaper than the market and well positioned for upside once it recovers.

Portfolio Review

Small Cap Opportunity Fund A Shares (without sales charge*) posted a return of -9.59% in first quarter 2025. Utilities was the only contributor among equity sectors; information technology, consumer discretionary and industrials were the largest detractors. The Fund underperformed the Russell 2000 Value Index in the period.

Leading contributors in the First Eagle Small Cap Opportunity Fund this quarter included H&E Equipment Services, Inc., Intrepid Potash, Inc. (IPI), Astronics Corporation (ATRO)(OTCPK:ATROB), Brookdale Senior Living Inc. (BKD) and EverQuote, Inc. Class A (EVER).

H&E Equipment Services is an integrated equipment services company that rents equipment, sells new and used equipment and parts, and provides repair and maintenance services. Shares of H&E were bolstered by a bidding war for the company by two other equipment-rental providers. H&E ultimately agreed to be acquired by Herc Holdings, one of the largest equipment rental companies in North America, at a significant premium in a stock-and-cash transition following an initial bid from United Rentals. This transaction is expected to close mid-2025.

Intrepid Potash is the only US producer of potash, with assets in Utah and New Mexico. The company reported production growth and sales volume improvement for its most recent quarter. Higher potash prices also were a tailwind during the quarter, as were the appointment of a new chief executive officer in December and the changes to the company’s corporate governance following the involvement of an activist investor. We continue to like the company’s unique, long-lived assets and its focus on increasing production capacity.

Astronics provides lighting and electronics for the interior of military and commercial aircraft. The company reported better-than-expected results for its most recent quarter, driven by strong demand from the commercial aerospace market. A patent dispute between Astronics and Lufthansa moved toward reaching a settlement during the quarter, which removed an overhang on the stock.

Brookdale Senior Living operates and manages 647 senior living communities in 41 US states. The company has made significant progress in recovering from significant pandemic-related disruptions, with improved occupancy rates and revenue per available unit. In late 2024, the company announced that it had amended its master lease arrangement with real estate investment trust Ventas; Brookdale will continue to lease 65 high-performing communities while relinquishing 55 and expects improvements in its cash flow as a result. We believe the company is well positioned to generate ongoing improvements in cash flow and earnings growth as it increases its owned real estate portfolio.

EverQuote operates online platforms to connect customers with auto insurance carriers and agents. The company reported better-than-expected results for its most recent quarter, driven by a recovery in its primary auto insurance category, growth in home and renters’ insurance, operational efficiencies and cost management. While a rise in claims, inflation and inadequate premiums had weighed on auto insurers advertising budgets for much of 2021–23, the recovery that emerged in 2024 should continue to support digital advertising spend to the potential benefit of EverQuote.

The leading detractors in the quarter were Ultra Clean Holdings, Inc. (UCTT), Graham Corporation (GHM), Ameresco, Inc. Class A (AMRC), International Money Express, Inc. (IMXI) and Beazer Homes USA, Inc (BZH).

Ultra Clean Holdings develops and supplies components, parts and subsystems for ultra-high-purity cleaning and analytical services for the semiconductor industry. Shares traded lower during the quarter on tariff concerns and as management acknowledged weak

demand and inventory absorption in China, reversing previous strong guidance. We think the company is well positioned to benefit from the longer-term growth of advanced nodes and improved operational efficiencies.

Graham manufactures mission-critical fluid, power, heat transfer and vacuum technologies for the defense, space, energy and process industries. In addition to a CEO transition announced during the quarter, concerns over exposure to long-term government contracts—which provided stability in other political climates— weighed on shares. We believe Graham’s underlying business remains strong despite the pullback in the stock price during the quarter and are upbeat about its ability to benefit from its late-2023 acquisition of P3 Technologies. We also like Graham’s disciplined approach to balancing growth initiatives with returning cash to shareholders through stock buybacks.

Ameresco is a clean-tech integrator and renewable energy company leveraging smart technology to help governments, and commercial and industrial clients decarbonize and build energy resilience throughout North America and Europe. Fears of contract cancellations due to potential federal budget cuts and less environmentally friendly policies under the Trump administration continued to pressure shares. With sophisticated solutions, innovative technologies and strong customer relationships, Ameresco appears well positioned for secular success.

International Money Express, a payment remittance company, processes money transfers between the US and Latin America/ Caribbean through both physical and electronic delivery. Investors were disappointed by the recent suspension of the company’s assessment of strategic alternatives; this review of opportunities to maximize shareholder value may have resulted in the sale of the business at a premium to the current market price. The company is well positioned to return capital to shareholders even as it continues to digitize operations for competitive positioning and margin expansion.

Beazer Homes is one of the largest homebuilders in the US, serving entry-level buyers primarily in the West and Southeast. Shares traded down during the quarter on weaker order volumes and margin pressure as rate promotions coincided with still-high interest rates. We remain constructive on the shares; secular prospects for home-builders remain strong given the severe undersupply of homes in the US, and Beazer is well positioned to grow earnings as it increases penetration.

We appreciate your confidence and thank you for your support.

Sincerely,

First Eagle Investments

data.title: HGLB: Solid CEF With Blended Portfolio Strategy
data.description:
Overview

Highland Global Allocation Fund (NYSE:HGLB) operates as a closed end fund that aims to provide attractive total returns through its blended portfolio of equities and debt-focused securities. This blended approach can be an efficient way to offset the volatility experienced from traditional equities, while also putting an emphasis on income generation. HGLB also differentiates itself by providing some global exposure to markets outside of the US, which offer attractive growth prospects.

Looking at the performance over the last year, we can see how HGLB's price has increased by more than 20%. When including all distributions that were paid out to shareholders, the total return jumps up above 36.3% over the same period. The most appealing aspect of maintaining exposure to HGLB is the generous starting dividend yield of about 11.9%. Additionally, HGLB pays out its distributions on a monthly basis, which makes the fund an attractive choice for investors that are looking to create a sizable stream of supplemental income. However, the fund has an inconsistent history with its distribution payouts.

Despite the price seeing a recent run-up, HGLB still trades at a sizable discount to NAV valuation. Reporting documents indicate that HGLB has been efficiently managed over recent years, but I do not feel confident in the outlook under current macroeconomic conditions. The higher interest rate environment may keep HGLB's valuation suppressed and may cause the fund's performance to lag behind traditional indexes in the near term. I do believe that future interest rate cuts can serve as a positive catalyst for growth, however. So let's start by taking a look at the underlying fund strategy that HGLB implements to generate its earnings.

Fund Strategy

According to the most recent fact sheet, HGLB has total net assets of $284M that is spread across 64 different holdings. HGLB strategy is to invest in US and foreign equities and fixed income securities as a way to provide attractive capital appreciation and income generation. The fixed income portion of the portfolio is likely allocated towards different debt securities that can generate interest income for the fund. The fact sheet highlights the fund's composition as follows:

  • Equities: 73.2%
  • Floating Rate Debt: 11.7%
  • MLPs (Master Limited Partnerships): 7.3%
  • High Yield Bonds: 5.2%
  • Other: 2.6%

From a sector breakdown, we can see that Energy investments account for the largest bulk of assets at 28.13%. This is followed by exposure to Real Estate and Consumer Cyclical investments, accounting for 26.30% and 10.23% of assets respectively. The combination of these top three sectors makes up nearly 65% of assets, so we can assume that these will have the greatest impact on HGLB's daily price changes and overall total return potential.

Most notably, the energy sector is an investment area that is usually associated with more consistent and easy to predict cash flow levels. Energy companies provide essential services that most people depend on for a high quality of life. Therefore, these can be businesses that are more suited to navigate all types of market conditions. On the other hand, real estate and consumer cyclical investments share some sensitivity to higher interest rates and shifts in consumer spending. Therefore, the fund may demonstrate some weakness through periods of market uncertainty.

HGLB is unique because it can provide investors exposure to private companies as well. Looking at the top 5 investment exposures, we can see that the largest position is in MidWave Wireless, with a 26% equity position and a 9.5% debt exposure. MidWave Wireless is a private company and operates as one of the largest independent wireless spectrum license holders in the US. We can also see exposure to public companies such as Energy Transfer (ET) and NexPoint Real Estate Finance (NXDT), which make up 7.4% and 7% of HGLB's assets respectively.

Valuation

Since HGLB operates as a closed end fund, the price can trade at a difference from the actual value of the underlying assets within. Therefore, HGLB currently trades at a large discount to NAV of nearly 26%. However, the fund has traded at an average discount to NAV of about 28.6% over the last three-year period, so the current valuation shouldn't be interpreted as a deal of any sort. Looking back on the fund's history below, we can see that the price has always traded in the discount territory, which could be a sign that there's an underlying flaw in its structure or investment approach. I've noticed that higher quality closed end funds can consistently trade at a premium to NAV, while the lower performing funds are more frequently in the discount territory.

In an effort to get better clarity on HGLB's performance, I reviewed the most recent annual report for the full year of 2024. Net investment income landed at $0.38 per share, while net realized gains amounted to $1.00 per share. Looking back on prior years, HGLB's level of net investment income has remained fairly consistent over the last few years, which is an indication that the underlying approach to generating income is efficient. Since the fund doesn't actually generate enough net investment income to fully cover its distributions by itself, HGLB is also reliant on net realized gains to fuel NAV growth and support payouts.

The issue that I have with this reliance on net realized gains, is that the performance is highly variable. After all, positive net realized gains are typically dependent on positive market conditions to provide growth. This is why the fund suffered from large net realized losses of $3.00 per share, as a result of the initial market drop related to the pandemic. Despite the dependence on net realized gains to fuel growth, HGLB has still been able to provide some measurable NAV growth over the last few years. For instance, here are the year-end NAV amounts:

  • 2020: $9.54 per share
  • 2021: $11.76 per share
  • 2022: $11.49 per share
  • 2023: $11.21 per share
  • 2024: 11.61 per share

Risk Profile

I believe that a big factor in HGLB's future success is the interest rate environment. HGLB originally launched back in 1988 as an open end mutual fund, but was converted to a closed end fund in early 2019. HGLB's timing was around the conversion was unfortunate because the panic happened shortly after. Following the pandemic, markets experienced shifting interest rates in response to record-breaking inflationary data. Since the fund has exposure to floating rate debt investments and sectors that are impacted by interest rates, such as real estate, I believe that valuation may be suppressed by the higher rates.

Looking at the chart below, we can see how HGLB has shared a bit of an inverse relationship with the federal funds rate. As interest rates were cut to near zero levels in response to the pandemic, the price of HGLB increased. A lower cost of borrowing made it easier for holdings to appreciation in value because operating margins were improved and due to stronger equity valuations. Conversely, HGLB's price started to decline throughout 2022 and 2023 when interest rates were being increased. A higher cost of debt negatively impacted operating margins for floating rate debt investments, making it harder for these borrowers to experience growth.

Therefore, future interest rate cuts could potentially serve as a growth catalyst in the future. However, it doesn't seem like markets are aligned for cuts at the moment. For instance, the Fed has decided to keep interest rates unchanged during their last meeting, as they continue to observe macroeconomic data. The impact of President Trump's tariffs are still to be determined, but I anticipate that a rising cost of goods will cause economic slowdown, higher inflationary data, and negative shifts in consumer spending. Therefore, there's a risk that HGLB's valuation remains suppressed because of inconsistent earnings and distribution coverage remains uncertain until rate cuts are in the picture.

Dividend

As of the latest declared monthly distribution of $0.088 per share, the current dividend yield sits around 11.9%. If we multiply this monthly payout by twelve, we get an estimated annual payout amount of $1.056 per share. Referring back to the same annual report, HGLB has a bit of an inconsistent history of being able to generate earnings in excess of the distribution amount. For instance, here are fund's annual earnings amounts over the last few years:

  • 2020: ($2.57) per share
  • 2021: $3.20 per share
  • 2022: $0.67 per share
  • 2023: $0.72 per share
  • 2024: $1.38 per share

Therefore, we can see that HGLB has only been able to out earn its distribution amount in two of the last five years. Despite 2021's performance leaving the fund with a lot of excess, I would like to see earnings consistently out earn the distributions every year. Looking back over the dividend history, we can see that HGLB's payout history has been frequently adjusted over its lifetime. Distributions were reduced during the pandemic between 2020 and 2022, but then they were slightly increased between 2022 and 2024. These frequent adjustments may not be ideal for investors that prefer to have a reliable and consistent stream of monthly income.

It's also worth noting that the same annual report indicates that a significant portion of the distributions are being funded by return of capital distributions. For instance, the annual distribution for 2024 amounted to $0.98 per share. Of this amount, only $0.37 per share was funded by net investment income, while the remaining $0.61 per share was funded by return of capital. The use of return of capital is an indication that the fund is struggling to generate a sufficient number of earnings to support distributions, which is detrimental to the fund's NAV. Return of capital distributions directly impacts the NAV, which limits growth potential.

Takeaway

In conclusion, I rate HGLB as a hold due to the inconsistent earnings and questionable distribution coverage. While I think the fund has demonstrated some positive qualities, such as the ability to preserve the NAV during unfavorable market conditions, I do not think that the current macroeconomic environment supports this sort of strategy. A large portion of the fund's assets are allocated towards sectors that are sensitive to interest rates. I do like the fact that HGLB can provide exposure to private companies, but I will remain on the sidelines until the interest rate environment improves.

data.title: Medpace: An Excellent Company In A Context Of Uncertainty
data.description:
Investment Thesis

I grant Medpace (NASDAQ:MEDP) a hold rating. In my opinion, the company has the best financial performance of all CRO firms that I have analyzed. It has high profit margin ratios, a low debt-to-equity ratio, and has experienced rapid growth over the last five years. However, in the short term, the CRO market expects headwinds. In fact, the company expects a 0.7% drop in its EBITDA. Due to the excellent performance achieved by Medpace, the company has high valuation ratios. Consequently, in a context of low growth, the truth that the market has placed on Medpace could disappear, which would cause a decline in the company's stock price.

The current macroeconomic environment in the health market is a serious risk for the company. U.S. President Trump signed an order mandating drugmakers to reduce the selling price of their medicines. The president expects a 50%-90% decline in the selling price, but it is not clear if he is bluffing. If this event occurs, it will undoubtedly have negative consequences for the CRO companies. In addition, the U.S. Government plans to implement massive cuts in healthcare expenses, which is also negative for CRO companies. It is not clear how deep these cuts will be. Due to this context, I rate Medpace with a hold rating. It is time to wait and see how the macroeconomic context evolves.

Introduction

On February 5, 2025, I wrote my first Medpace write-up, granting the firm a strong buy. In my opinion, Medpace has an excellent financial position and a good business model. However, the current macroeconomic environment of uncertainty is a serious threat for the future revenues of the company. This article will delve into both aspects. In my previous article, I wrote about the business model of the company.

Profitability

Medpace has outstanding profitability ratios compared to its peers in the CRO and CDMO markets, as depicted in the following table. For instance, the company reported a 68.08% gross profit ratio, which is relatively higher than the percentages reported by its peers. In addition, the company has a 68.82% return on equity, the highest value by a large margin among the peer group.

To achieve these excellent results, Medpace must have done something different from the other firms. On the contrary, it should have reported lower profitability ratios.

Target Market

Medpace concentrates its operations in small biopharmaceutical companies. In 2024, 79% of its revenues came from small biopharmaceutical firms, while mid-sized biopharmaceutical companies accounted for 17% of its revenues, as shown in the image below. Consequently, the company does not have large pharmaceutical firms, as its primary clients. In fact, these companies accounted for only 4% of the revenues in 2024.

I would like to compare Medpace with ICON PLC (ICLR). Unlike Medpace, Icon's revenues come from a small number of large pharmaceutical companies. For instance, in 2023, the largest 10 clients of Icon accounted for 41.4% of its revenues, as depicted in the following image. Due to this strategy, Icon has a lower gross profit margin. In short, Medpace has been successful in offering its services to small and mid-sized biopharmaceutical companies, a segment that other CROs and CDMOs do not typically target.

Infrastructure

The infrastructure is another strength that Medpace has. The company has the infrastructure to provide in-house services and products in the four phases of clinical research. For instance, it has laboratories to provide imaging, cardiovascular, and testing services in different areas. It also has bioanalytical laboratories for researching and developing drug candidates. Furthermore, it manages clinical trials and recruits patients for its client tests, among other services.

Companies, such as IQVIA and ICON, also offer the previous services, to name a few. However, as commented in the previous section, Medpace provides these services to small and mid-cap companies. Meanwhile, its competitors target large pharmaceutical companies. In other words, Medpace offers top-notch services to small firms.

Medpace concentrates some of its services on complex therapeutic areas that have low success rates in developing medical drugs. To provide context, the oncology, metabolic, cardiovascular, and CNS therapeutic areas accounted for 73% of Medpace's revenues in 2024. Among these therapeutic segments, oncology, metabolic, and CNS have relatively low success rates in developing medical treatments.

Therefore, they will prefer to collaborate with a CRO company that has expertise in those areas. Developing medical treatments takes years, so these companies will not risk their money developing their products on their own, or hiring CRO companies with less experience than Medpace.

Macroeconomic Context

The healthcare market in the U.S. has a negative outlook. The U.S. Government expects to reduce its expenditure on health. Recently, the Trump administration released a project aimed at reducing Medicaid expenditures by $700 billion over the next ten years.

Moving to the United States Department of Health and Human Services (HHS), the U.S. Government expects to cut the HHS budgets from $128 billion in 2025 to $95 billion in 2026, for a 25% drop. With this decision, fewer resources will be allocated to the health system.

The U.S. President signed an executive order that mandates pharmaceutical and biopharmaceutical companies to lower their medicine selling prices. The intention is that American drugmakers sell medicines at similar prices to those in other countries. In a press conference, U.S. President Donald Trump said that he expects cuts of 59% and 90% in the U.S. medicine prices.

The policies discussed in this section are some of the most relevant decisions. In addition, the U.S. Government administration has implemented other decisions to reduce health expenses.

My Opinion: Macroeconomic Context

I would like to express my opinion regarding the previous section. The U.S. Government expects to reduce $4.5 trillion in tax expenses between 2025 and 2034. With this decision, it intends to boost the economy. However, to implement this measure, it must cut its expenses in other areas. One of those areas is healthcare. In one way or another, the current situation is a zero-sum game. The U.S. Government, as well as any other country, does not have infinite resources. Consequently, to implement reforms, it must reduce its expenses in the healthcare system. If this scenario occurs, the CRO market will be negatively affected in the U.S. There would be fewer resources allocated to the health sector. Therefore, the biopharma sector will reduce the development of new medicines since the demand for new medicines will decline.

Regarding the executive order that mandates drugmakers to reduce the selling price of their medicines, I think that the U.S. President could be bluffing. It could be a strategy of hard pressure with high exigencies at the beginning, and then softening these demands to obtain an agreement that is better than the initial position. For instance, he could reach an agreement mandating pharmaceutical companies to lower the selling price of their medicine by 10%. Nonetheless, any reduction in the medicine selling prices will be negative for Medpace.

Valuation

This section evaluates the possible evolution of Medpace's EV/EBITDA. I assume two bearish scenarios aligned to the current context of uncertainty, and a neutral scenario aligned to the company's historical growth rate. The three scenarios are:

  1. The EBITDA will decline by 0.7% in 2025 and then will increase at a 3% CAGR between 2026 and 2030.
  2. The EBITDA will decline by 0.7% in 2025 and then will increase at a 13% CAGR between 2026 and 2030.
  3. The EBITDA will decline by 0.7% in 2025 and then will increase at a 21.64% CAGR between 2026 and 2030.

In the three cases, I use the company's guidance for 2025. Medpace projects that its EBITDA will drop by 0.7% this year. The scenarios only differ in the CAGR used for the period of 2026-2030. In the first scenario, the EV/EBITDA would reach 15.91 in 2030, as depicted in the following chart. This scenario assumes a low growth in Medpace's EBITDA, which is aligned with the U.S. inflation growth rate. Meanwhile, in the second scenario, the EV/EBITDA would reach 10.01 in 2030, which is still high. I think that in both scenarios, the market capitalization of Medpace would decline.

Medpace's EBITDA grew at a 21.64% CAGR between 2016 and 2024, which is its historical growth rate. Due to this reason, I decided to use this rate in the third scenario to forecast the EBITDA (base scenario). In the last scenario, the EV/EBITDA ratio would reach 6.93 in 2030, which, I think, is an appealing ratio. If the EBITDA grows by 21.64% annually, it justifies the current 18.33 EV/EBITDA ratio of the company.

The only negative aspects are the valuation ratios. They suggest that the company is overvalued. However, companies that perform well tend to have high valuation ratios.

Conclusion

In light of the analysis presented in this article, I believe it is time to hold the position and wait until the macroeconomic environment of uncertainty dissipates. The U.S. Government is in the endeavor to reduce its healthcare expenses. In addition, U.S. President Donald Trump signed an executive order mandating drugmakers in the U.S. to reduce the selling price of their medicines. However, it is not clear how deep the cut should be. The President has outlined a 50%-90% reduction for some medications. He may be bluffing, with the scope to reach a deal that mandates drugmakers to reduce the selling price of the medicine by a lower magnitude than those commented on before.

From a financial perspective, in my personal opinion, Medpace is an excellent company. I think it is the best CRO company that I have analyzed. It has exceptional profitability margins. In addition, the firm has experienced a fast growth rate and has a low debt-to-equity ratio. It also has a business model concentrated on providing CRO services to small and mid-sized biopharmaceutical companies, a market segment that is overlooked by other CRO companies. Finally, Medpace has expertise in complicated therapeutic areas, such as oncology.

Due to the current environment of uncertainty, this year will be a challenging one for Medpace. The company projects a 0.7% decline in its EBITDA. Taking all these factors into consideration, I think it is time to be patient and see how the current macroeconomic environment of uncertainty unfolds.
